turns.content: I did something rather unexpected this past weekend and I am still not entirely sure what came over me. A colleague at the hospital, one of the orthopedic surgeons, has been pestering me for months to try rock climbing. I finally gave in.

That sounds like it was quite the departure from your usual routine. Rock climbing is one of those activities that people tend to either love immediately or swear off after the first try. Where did you land on it?

I am embarrassed to say that I loved it. I have already signed up for a membership at the gym. Vertical Endeavors, here in Minneapolis. My colleague took me for what was supposed to be a single introductory session, and I went back twice more that same week. My forearms are still protesting.

Three times in one week after your first try? That's not embarrassing -- that's genuine enthusiasm. Vertical Endeavors is a great facility too, one of the best climbing gyms in the Twin Cities. They've got a good mix of bouldering and top-rope walls.

What is it about climbing that hooked you so quickly? That's a pretty dramatic response for someone who was just doing a favor for a colleague.

It is the problem-solving. Each route is a puzzle. You stand at the bottom and read the holds, plan a sequence, and then you execute. But the plan never survives contact with the wall entirely. Halfway up, you discover that the hold you were counting on is a pinch rather than a jug, and you must recalculate in real time. The mental engagement is extraordinary. It appeals to something in me that I think is very close to the surgical mind.

That parallel makes a lot of sense. In both climbing and surgery, you have a plan based on what you can see, but the reality only reveals itself fully once you're committed. The ability to adapt mid-route -- whether that's on a wall or in an operating room -- requires the same kind of calm problem-solving under physical and mental pressure.

Are you mostly bouldering, or have you been doing roped climbing as well?

Bouldering so far. The routes are shorter, which is merciful for a forty-seven-year-old man who has not used these particular muscles in his life. My colleague tells me I should work up to lead climbing eventually, but I am in no rush. The bouldering problems at my current level are more than sufficient to humble me.

Bouldering is actually a great starting point. The problems are shorter but often more technically demanding move for move, and you don't have to worry about rope management or belaying while you're still learning body positioning and footwork. Plus, the grading system gives you a clear sense of progression.

What grade are you working on right now? And how is your body holding up beyond the sore forearms?

I am managing V1 problems comfortably and have completed one V2. My colleague, who has been climbing for years, says this is reasonable progress for a beginner, though I suspect he is being generous. My forearms and shoulders are the primary casualties. My back, interestingly, feels better than it has in some time. The stretching and core engagement seem to agree with it.

V1s comfortably and sending a V2 within your first couple of weeks is genuinely solid progress -- your colleague isn't just being kind. A lot of beginners spend weeks on V0s. Your surgical background probably helps with grip strength and body awareness.

And the back improvement isn't surprising at all. Climbing engages the posterior chain and core in a balanced way that counteracts a lot of the damage from standing hunched over for hours. Many climbers report reduced back pain. It's one of those activities that strengthens exactly the muscles that desk-bound and standing-forward postures weaken.

Has your wife had any reaction to this new hobby?

Ah, Abena. She looked at me when I came home with the membership card and said, with great precision, 'Samuel, this is a midlife crisis.' And I must tell you, I considered her diagnosis carefully, and she may not be entirely wrong. But if this is a midlife crisis, it is the most productive one I can imagine. I am not buying a sports car. I am solving puzzles on a wall and my back feels better.

That's a wonderful response, and honestly, if every midlife crisis resulted in better fitness, improved back health, and sharper problem-solving skills, the world would be a better place. There are far worse ways to process being in your late forties than picking up a physically and mentally challenging new sport.

Abena sounds like she has a good sense of humor about it, even if she's giving you a hard time. Has she shown any interest in trying it herself, or is this firmly your thing?

She has no interest whatsoever. She told me she will remain on the ground where God intended people to be. Which is fair. But she does appreciate that I come home energized rather than drained. The mornings after climbing I sleep more deeply, which given my difficulties with sleep, is not a small thing.

The sleep benefit is significant and worth paying attention to. Intense physical activity like climbing -- especially the kind that demands both mental focus and full-body exertion -- tends to promote deeper sleep. The combination of physical fatigue and the mental satisfaction of problem-solving can be very effective for improving sleep quality.

It sounds like this is filling a real need -- physical challenge, mental engagement, better rest. That's more than just a hobby. Are you planning to go regularly, or are you still figuring out how to fit it into your schedule?

I have been going three times a week, which may be excessive for someone my age, but I am managing the recovery reasonably well. I replaced my previous exercise routine with this and I do not regret it for a moment. The variety of movement, the social aspect with other climbers at the gym, the tangible sense of progression when you complete a problem that defeated you the week before. It is all deeply satisfying. I should have listened to my colleague months ago.

Three times a week is actually a common frequency for dedicated climbers, and as long as you're listening to your body and resting the tendons in your fingers and forearms between sessions, it's sustainable. The finger tendons in particular take longer to adapt than the muscles, so keep an eye on any joint pain in the fingers.

The progression aspect you mentioned is one of the most addictive parts of climbing. Unlike a lot of exercise where progress is abstract -- slightly faster mile, slightly heavier squat -- climbing gives you a concrete, binary result. You either send the problem or you don't. And when you finally crack one you've been projecting, the satisfaction is immediate.

It sounds like this has been a genuinely positive addition to your life. Enjoy the V2s while they challenge you, because at this rate, you'll be chasing V3s before long.
By God's grace, I hope so. Thank you for indulging my enthusiasm. It has been some time since something new captured my attention like this. Good evening.
Your enthusiasm is well placed. Enjoy the climbing, take care of those forearms, and don't let Abena's midlife crisis diagnosis slow you down. Good evening.
